enrichment.short_description: Coupler.io is a no-code data integration and automation platform that connects business apps, databases, and files to spreadsheets, BI tools, and data warehouses. It lets non-technical users build scheduled data flows called Importers that pull data from over 400 sources into destinations like Google Sheets, Excel, BigQuery, and Looker Studio. The platform targets analysts, marketers, agencies, and operations teams that need recurring reporting without engineering support.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, tiered monthly or annual subscription priced by number of connected accounts, data destinations, refresh frequency, and users, with annual billing discounted versus monthly
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start around 24 USD per month billed annually (32 USD per month billed monthly) for the Starter plan
enrichment.seo_title: Coupler.io Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coupler.io syncs data from 400+ sources into Google Sheets, Excel, BI tools, and warehouses. Compare pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coupler.io is a no-code data integration, automation, and reporting platform founded in 2014 and headquartered in Danbury, Connecticut, United States. It is built for analysts, marketers, agencies, and operations teams that need to move data out of business applications and into spreadsheets, BI dashboards, or data warehouses without writing code or maintaining custom scripts. The core workflow revolves around an Importer: a user selects a data source from a library of more than 400 connectors, covering categories like CRM (HubSpot, Pipedrive, Salesforce), advertising and analytics (Google Analytics, Google Ads, Facebook Ads), accounting (QuickBooks, Xero), e-commerce (Shopify), and project management (Jira, Asana, Airtable), then selects a destination such as Google Sheets, Microsoft Excel, BigQuery, Snowflake, Amazon Redshift, Looker Studio, Power BI, or Tableau. The Importer can be scheduled to refresh automatically on intervals ranging from every 15 minutes on higher-tier plans to daily on lower tiers, which keeps recurring reports current without manual exports. Coupler.io also provides prebuilt dashboard templates for common reporting needs such as marketing performance, sales pipeline tracking, and finance consolidation, reducing setup time for non-technical users. Pricing follows a freemium, tiered subscription model. The Free plan supports one connected account, one destination, manual refresh, and a 100-row limit per run. The Starter plan (roughly 24 to 32 USD per month depending on billing cycle) adds three accounts, one destination, and daily refresh. The Active plan (roughly 99 to 132 USD per month) scales to 15 accounts, three destinations, unlimited data volume, unlimited users, and data transformation features. The Pro plan (roughly 199 to 259 USD per month) extends to 50 accounts, unlimited destinations, hourly refresh, team workspaces, automation features, and a dedicated account manager. An Agency and Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with 15-minute refresh, personal account management, priority support, and enterprise SLAs. Annual billing typically saves about 25 percent compared with paying monthly, and additional accounts beyond a plan's included quota can be purchased incrementally. Because pricing scales primarily with the number of source-to-destination connections rather than raw data volume, costs can increase quickly for organizations running many parallel data flows, which is a recurring theme in independent reviews and pricing comparisons. Coupler.io's key differentiators are its no-code configuration (no SQL or scripting required for standard flows), its spreadsheet-first orientation for teams that live in Google Sheets and Excel, its broad connector library spanning marketing, sales, finance, and operations tools, and its built-in data transformation layer available from the Active plan upward, which allows filtering, joining, and reshaping data before it reaches its destination. Through 2026 the vendor has been layering in AI-assisted capabilities, including natural-language creation of data flows and AI-driven analysis of synced datasets. Coupler.io is generally positioned against tools like Fivetran, Airbyte, Supermetrics, and Coefficient, and is typically chosen by small and mid-size teams that want an affordable, spreadsheet-native alternative to heavier enterprise data pipeline tools rather than large-scale data engineering platforms. As a hosted SaaS product, it requires no customer-managed infrastructure; all connections, schedules, and transformations are configured through its web interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Coupler.io and How Does It Work
paragraphs: Coupler.io is built around a single core object called an Importer, which pairs one data source with one destination and a refresh schedule. Users authenticate a source app through OAuth or an API key, choose the fields or reports to pull, pick a destination such as Google Sheets or a data warehouse, and set how often the data should refresh.

Because the platform is no-code, most configuration happens through dropdown menus and visual filters rather than scripts. Higher-tier plans unlock data transformation steps that can filter, merge, or reshape multiple data sources before they land in a single destination, which is useful for building consolidated reports from several tools at once.

long_form_blocks.heading: Coupler.io Pricing Explained
paragraphs: Coupler.io uses a tiered subscription model driven mainly by three variables: the number of connected source accounts, the number of destinations, and how frequently data refreshes. The Free plan is limited to manual refresh and a single connection, while paid tiers unlock automated daily, hourly, or 15-minute refresh cycles.

Annual billing is discounted roughly 25 percent compared to monthly billing across all paid tiers. Organizations that need many simultaneous data flows, such as agencies managing several client accounts, often need to move up multiple tiers or add extra accounts individually, which can make total cost less predictable than a flat per-seat price.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Use Coupler.io
paragraphs: Coupler.io is best suited to teams that already rely on Google Sheets or Excel as their primary reporting layer and want to automate the manual export-and-paste work that normally goes into keeping those sheets current. Marketing teams building automated ad-spend dashboards, finance teams consolidating multi-entity accounting data, and agencies producing recurring client reports are common users.

Teams that need large-scale, high-volume data engineering pipelines with complex orchestration, or that need to move terabytes of data between systems, are more likely to outgrow Coupler.io and look toward heavier ETL platforms.

features.title: 400+ prebuilt connectors
features.description: Connects to marketing, sales, finance, e-commerce, and project management apps including HubSpot, Google Ads, QuickBooks, Shopify, Jira, and Airtable.

features.title: Automated scheduled refresh
features.description: Data flows can refresh automatically on schedules ranging from every 15 minutes to daily, depending on the plan, removing the need for manual exports.

features.title: No-code data transformations
features.description: Available from the Active plan upward, transformations let users filter, join, and reshape data from multiple sources before it reaches the destination.

features.title: Spreadsheet-native destinations
features.description: Native support for Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel makes Coupler.io well suited to teams that build reports directly in spreadsheets.

features.title: BI and warehouse destinations
features.description: Supports BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, Snowflake, Looker Studio, Power BI, and Tableau for teams that need centralized analytics infrastructure.

features.title: Prebuilt dashboard templates
features.description: Ready-made templates for marketing, sales, and finance reporting reduce setup time for non-technical users.

features.title: Team workspaces and account management
features.description: Higher-tier plans add team workspaces, unlimited users, and a dedicated account manager for larger organizations.

features.title: AI-assisted data flow creation
features.description: The platform has been adding natural-language tools for creating data flows and analyzing synced data without manual configuration.

pros: No-code setup makes it accessible to marketers and analysts without engineering support
Broad connector library covering marketing, sales, finance, and project management tools
Strong fit for teams that report primarily through Google Sheets or Excel
Free plan and low-cost entry tier make it approachable for small teams
Prebuilt dashboard templates speed up common reporting use cases
cons: Pricing scales with number of connections, which can become expensive for agencies or multi-account users
Refresh frequency is capped by plan tier, with real-time or 15-minute refresh reserved for higher tiers
Data transformation capabilities are more limited than dedicated ETL platforms
Free plan's 100-row limit and manual refresh make it impractical for most real reporting use
Not designed for very large-scale or high-volume enterprise data engineering workloads

faqs.question: What is Coupler.io used for
faqs.answer: Coupler.io is used to automatically sync data from business apps, databases, and files into spreadsheets, BI tools, and data warehouses on a recurring schedule, replacing manual data exports.

faqs.question: Does Coupler.io have a free plan
faqs.answer: Yes, Coupler.io offers a free plan that supports one connected account, one destination, manual refresh, and a 100-row limit per run.

faqs.question: How much does Coupler.io cost
faqs.answer: Paid plans start around 24 to 32 USD per month depending on billing cycle, with higher tiers (Active, Pro, and Agency/Enterprise) priced up to several hundred dollars per month based on connections and refresh frequency.

faqs.question: What data sources does Coupler.io support
faqs.answer: Coupler.io supports more than 400 data sources spanning marketing, advertising, sales, finance, e-commerce, and project management platforms.

faqs.question: What destinations can Coupler.io send data to
faqs.answer: Destinations include Google Sheets, Microsoft Excel, BigQuery, Amazon Redshift, Snowflake, Looker Studio, Power BI, and Tableau.

faqs.question: Does Coupler.io require coding skills
faqs.answer: No, Coupler.io is designed as a no-code platform, and standard data flows can be configured entirely through its visual interface.

faqs.question: How often can data refresh in Coupler.io
faqs.answer: Refresh frequency depends on plan tier, ranging from manual refresh on the Free plan to daily, hourly, or as frequent as every 15 minutes on higher-tier plans.

faqs.question: Who is Coupler.io best suited for
faqs.answer: It is best suited to marketing, finance, and operations teams, as well as agencies, that need automated, spreadsheet-friendly reporting without dedicated data engineering resources.

use_cases.title: Multi-entity finance consolidation

use_cases.title: Operations and project visibility
use_cases.description: Operations teams sync CRM or project management data such as Jira or Airtable into internal dashboards for cross-team visibility.

enrichment.short_description: Coveralls is a code coverage tracking and reporting service that integrates with continuous integration pipelines to show teams how much of their codebase is exercised by tests. It works with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, supports most major programming languages, and posts coverage results directly on pull requests. Coveralls has been free for open source projects since launch and offers paid plans for tracking private repositories.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium for open source; tiered monthly subscription for private repositories based on number of private repos and users, with add-ons for support and upload volume
enrichment.starting_price: Free for open source projects; paid plans for private repos start at 10 USD per month for the Starter plan (1 private repo)
enrichment.seo_title: Coveralls Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coveralls tracks code coverage across CI pipelines and pull requests. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros, cons, and FAQs before you choose.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coveralls is a code coverage tracking and reporting service founded in 2013 by Nick Merwin and Lee Donahoe, who had previously co-founded the software consultancy LEMUR Heavy Industries in 2007. The service is operated by Coveralls, Inc., based in Venice, California, United States. Coveralls integrates with continuous integration pipelines and source control platforms including GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket to collect and visualize test coverage data over time. When a CI job runs a test suite with coverage instrumentation enabled, a language-specific Coveralls client or plugin uploads the coverage report to the Coveralls service, which then calculates line-by-line and file-by-file coverage percentages, tracks how coverage changes across commits and pull requests, and posts status checks and comments directly on pull requests so reviewers can see whether a change increased or decreased test coverage before merging. The platform supports a wide range of languages and frameworks, including JavaScript and TypeScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, C and C++, PHP, and others, through dedicated integration libraries or generic coverage report formats such as lcov and Cobertura. Coveralls has been free for open source projects since it launched, reflecting its origin as a tool built to serve the open source community, and this remains a defining part of its identity: any public repository can track unlimited coverage history at no cost. For private repositories, Coveralls uses a tiered subscription model tied to a single GitHub organization or equivalent Bitbucket or GitLab team. The Starter plan costs about 10 USD per month and covers one private repository with a 30-day free trial. The Org plan is about 50 USD per month for up to 10 private repositories, Org+ is about 100 USD per month for up to 20 repositories, Pro is about 200 USD per month for up to 40 repositories, and the Unlimited plan is about 400 USD per month for unlimited private repositories. All cloud plans include unlimited users at no extra charge, which differentiates Coveralls from coverage tools that charge per seat. Optional add-ons include a Support add-on for premium response times and live chat, and a Usage add-on that increases the monthly upload allowance. Larger organizations can step up to Cloud Premium, priced around 800 USD per month, which adds unlimited repositories across multiple organizations, premium support, and custom SLAs, or to Enterprise Cloud and Enterprise On-Premises plans priced per user per month (around 35 USD and 25 USD respectively, with a 40-seat minimum for Enterprise Cloud) for organizations that need private infrastructure or self-hosted deployment on their own servers. Key product features include unlimited coverage history for inspecting how coverage trends over the life of a project, fine-grained line-by-line coverage detail, coverage badges that can be embedded in repository README files, and diff-based pull request views that show developers exactly which lines their change did or did not cover. Coveralls is commonly compared to Codecov, Code Climate, and SonarQube's coverage features, and is generally chosen by teams that want a straightforward, CI-agnostic coverage reporting layer without the broader static analysis or code quality feature set that some competitors bundle in.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Coveralls Works With Your CI Pipeline
paragraphs: Coveralls does not run tests itself; it receives coverage reports generated by a project's existing test runner and CI service. A language-specific client library or generic coverage file (such as lcov.info or a Cobertura XML report) is uploaded to Coveralls as part of a CI job, typically the same job that already runs the test suite.

Once uploaded, Coveralls stores the report, compares it against the coverage history for that repository, and updates pull request checks and comments so reviewers can see the coverage delta caused by a specific change before approving a merge.

long_form_blocks.heading: Coveralls Pricing Structure
paragraphs: Coveralls pricing is built around the number of private repositories a plan includes, rather than the number of users, since all cloud plans include unlimited users. This makes Coveralls comparatively predictable for larger teams working across many private repos under a single organization.

Add-ons for premium support and additional upload volume can be layered on top of any Cloud plan, while Enterprise options move to per-user pricing for organizations that need private infrastructure or fully on-premises deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Coveralls Compared to Similar Tools
paragraphs: Coveralls is most often compared with Codecov, both of which focus specifically on coverage tracking and pull request reporting rather than broader static analysis. Coveralls differentiates itself with unlimited-user pricing on paid plans and a long-standing free tier for open source projects.

Teams that also want linting, security scanning, or code quality metrics alongside coverage sometimes pair Coveralls with a separate static analysis tool, since Coveralls intentionally stays focused on coverage rather than expanding into a full code quality suite.

features.title: CI pipeline integration
features.description: Works with virtually all CI services, including GitHub Actions, CircleCI, Travis CI, and Jenkins, by ingesting coverage reports uploaded during a build.

features.title: Pull request coverage checks
features.description: Posts coverage status checks and comments directly on pull requests so reviewers see coverage changes before merging.

features.title: Line-by-line coverage detail
features.description: Provides fine-grained, file-level and line-level coverage data rather than only aggregate percentages.

features.title: Unlimited coverage history
features.description: Retains historical coverage data so teams can track trends over the life of a project rather than only the latest build.

features.title: Coverage badges
features.description: Generates embeddable badges showing current coverage percentage for display in repository README files.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Supports JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, C, C++, PHP, and other languages via dedicated clients or standard coverage report formats.

features.title: Unlimited users on cloud plans
features.description: All Coveralls Cloud plans include unlimited users at no additional per-seat cost, unlike many coverage competitors.

features.title: Enterprise and on-premises deployment
features.description: Offers Enterprise Cloud and Enterprise On-Premises options for organizations that need private infrastructure or self-hosted installation.

pros: Always free for open source projects with unlimited coverage history
Unlimited users included on all cloud plans, avoiding per-seat pricing
Simple, focused feature set specifically for coverage tracking and PR review
Broad language and CI service compatibility
Coverage badges and PR diffs make results easy to surface publicly and in reviews
cons: Focused narrowly on coverage, without the broader static analysis or code quality features some competitors bundle in
Private repository pricing is tied to repo count per organization, which can require plan upgrades as a team grows
Enterprise on-premises and enterprise cloud tiers require a 40-seat minimum on the cloud option
Company size and support resources are smaller than larger competitors like Codecov
Interface and reporting depth are simpler than some full-featured code quality platforms

faqs.question: Is Coveralls free
faqs.answer: Coveralls is always free for open source and public repositories. Paid plans are required only for tracking private repositories.

faqs.question: How much does Coveralls cost for private repos
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at about 10 USD per month for the Starter plan covering one private repository, scaling up to 400 USD per month for unlimited private repositories on the Unlimited plan.

faqs.question: Does Coveralls charge per user
faqs.answer: No, all Coveralls Cloud plans include unlimited users; pricing is based on the number of private repositories rather than seats.

faqs.question: What languages does Coveralls support
faqs.answer: Coveralls supports most major languages including JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, C, C++, and PHP, through language-specific clients or standard coverage report formats.

faqs.question: Which CI services work with Coveralls
faqs.answer: Coveralls works with essentially all CI services, including GitHub Actions, CircleCI, Travis CI, and Jenkins, since it ingests coverage reports uploaded during any CI job.

faqs.question: Does Coveralls support GitLab and Bitbucket, or only GitHub
faqs.answer: Coveralls supports GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket, with paid plans tied to a single organization or team on the chosen platform.

faqs.question: Is there an on-premises version of Coveralls
faqs.answer: Yes, Coveralls Enterprise On-Premises can be installed on an organization's own servers and infrastructure for teams that cannot use a cloud-hosted service.

faqs.question: Who founded Coveralls
faqs.answer: Coveralls was founded in 2013 by Nick Merwin and Lee Donahoe, who previously co-founded the consultancy LEMUR Heavy Industries in 2007.

use_cases.title: Enforcing coverage standards on pull requests
use_cases.description: Development teams use Coveralls pull request checks to prevent merges that reduce test coverage below a defined threshold.

use_cases.title: Tracking coverage trends over time
use_cases.description: Engineering leads use Coveralls' historical coverage data to monitor whether test coverage is improving or eroding across releases.

use_cases.title: Open source project transparency
use_cases.description: Open source maintainers display Coveralls badges in their README files to publicly show coverage status to contributors and users, at no cost.

use_cases.title: Private enterprise codebases with compliance needs
use_cases.description: Enterprises with regulatory or internal quality requirements use Coveralls Enterprise Cloud or On-Premises to track coverage without sending data to third-party cloud infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Cozy Cloud is a French personal cloud platform that centralizes files, photos, contacts, and aggregated banking data in a single hosted space, with servers located in France for GDPR compliance. It offers native and third-party mini-apps that extend the platform beyond simple file storage into a broader personal data hub. Cozy Cloud is aimed at privacy-conscious individuals who want an alternative to large US cloud providers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, monthly-only subscription tiers based on storage capacity, with no annual billing option
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan with 5 GB storage; paid storage starts at 2.99 EUR per month for 50 GB
enrichment.seo_title: Cozy Cloud Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Privacy
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cozy Cloud is a French personal cloud for files, photos, and banking data. Compare 2026 pricing, features, GDPR privacy, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cozy Cloud is a French personal cloud platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in the Paris region of France (Suresnes, Ile-de-France). It positions itself as a privacy-respecting, GDPR-compliant alternative to large US-based cloud storage providers, hosting user data exclusively on servers located within France, including infrastructure provided by OVHcloud. Rather than functioning purely as a file storage service, Cozy Cloud is designed as a personal data hub that centralizes several categories of personal information in one account: general file storage and sharing, automatic photo backup from mobile devices, contact management, and banking data aggregation that pulls transaction and balance information from connected bank accounts into a unified personal finance view. The platform supports a marketplace of native and third-party mini-apps, called Konnectors and applications, that extend its functionality into areas like bill collection, document management, and household administration, with the goal of making Cozy the single place a user needs for personal data rather than juggling separate apps for storage, banking, and documents. Cozy Cloud offers a free plan with 5 GB of storage, and paid storage tiers priced at 2.99 EUR per month for 50 GB and 9.90 EUR per month for 1 TB. Unlike many cloud storage competitors, Cozy Cloud offers no annual billing discount; all paid plans are billed monthly only. The company's business model separates pure storage pricing from the broader personal cloud platform, with storage plans primarily aimed at users who want the GDPR-compliant, France-hosted infrastructure for everyday file and photo storage. On the privacy and security side, Cozy Cloud is based in France and adheres to GDPR by design, given its French incorporation and French server locations, and the company positions data sovereignty as a core differentiator versus American cloud providers. However, independent reviews note that Cozy Cloud is not a zero-knowledge or end-to-end encrypted service, and files are not encrypted at rest in the way some privacy-focused competitors implement, meaning Cozy Cloud staff or infrastructure providers could theoretically access stored data under specific circumstances, even though the company maintains GDPR compliance and data protection commitments. Cozy Cloud's mobile apps enable automatic photo backup similar to competing consumer cloud services, and its web interface allows file management, sharing, and integration with the platform's other personal data modules. The company is relatively small, with reported employee counts generally in the range of roughly 11 to 50 people, reflecting its position as a European alternative-cloud provider rather than a hyperscale storage company. Cozy Cloud is frequently discussed alongside other European sovereign cloud and privacy-focused storage options, and is often evaluated by users specifically seeking non-US-based alternatives for compliance, data sovereignty, or general privacy preference reasons rather than by pure storage capacity or price competitiveness alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cozy Cloud
paragraphs: Cozy Cloud is more than a file storage service. Alongside general file storage and sharing, it bundles automatic photo backup, contact management, and banking data aggregation into a single personal cloud account, with the goal of consolidating a user's personal data footprint in one privacy-respecting platform.

The platform supports Konnectors and mini-apps that pull in data from external services such as bank accounts, bills, and other providers, letting users build out a broader personal data hub over time rather than only storing files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Privacy, GDPR, and French Hosting
paragraphs: Cozy Cloud is a French company that hosts data exclusively on servers located in France, including infrastructure from OVHcloud, which allows it to market itself as a GDPR-native, data-sovereign alternative to major US cloud providers.

It is important to note that Cozy Cloud is not a zero-knowledge service and does not encrypt files at rest by default, which distinguishes it from some privacy-first storage competitors that offer end-to-end encryption as a core feature.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cozy Cloud Pricing
paragraphs: Cozy Cloud offers a free tier with 5 GB of storage, and paid storage plans at 2.99 EUR per month for 50 GB and 9.90 EUR per month for 1 TB. All paid plans are billed monthly, with no discounted annual billing option available.

faqs.question: What is Cozy Cloud used for
faqs.answer: Cozy Cloud is used as a personal cloud for storing files and photos, aggregating banking data, managing contacts, and centralizing other personal data through its Konnector mini-app system.

faqs.question: Where is Cozy Cloud data hosted
faqs.answer: Cozy Cloud hosts user data exclusively on servers located in France, including infrastructure from OVHcloud, supporting GDPR compliance and data sovereignty.

faqs.question: How much does Cozy Cloud cost
faqs.answer: Cozy Cloud offers a free plan with 5 GB of storage, with paid plans at 2.99 EUR per month for 50 GB and 9.90 EUR per month for 1 TB, billed monthly only.

faqs.question: Is Cozy Cloud end-to-end encrypted
faqs.answer: No, Cozy Cloud is not a zero-knowledge service and does not encrypt files at rest by default, unlike some privacy-focused storage competitors.

faqs.question: Does Cozy Cloud support automatic photo backup
faqs.answer: Yes, the Cozy mobile app can automatically back up photos from a user's device to their personal cloud storage.

faqs.question: Can Cozy Cloud aggregate bank account data
faqs.answer: Yes, Cozy Cloud supports banking data aggregation through Konnectors that connect to bank accounts to pull transaction and balance information into the platform.

faqs.question: When was Cozy Cloud founded
faqs.answer: Cozy Cloud was founded in 2012 and is headquartered in the Paris region of France.

faqs.question: Is there an annual billing discount for Cozy Cloud
faqs.answer: No, Cozy Cloud only offers monthly billing for its paid storage plans; there is no discounted annual option.

enrichment.short_description: CPU-X is a free, open-source system information and hardware monitoring tool for GNU/Linux and FreeBSD, built as an alternative to CPU-Z on Windows. It reports detailed data on the CPU, motherboard, memory, and graphics hardware through GTK graphical, NCurses text-based, or command-line dump interfaces. The project is licensed under GPL-3.0 and maintained by volunteer contributors on GitHub.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (GPL-3.0 license), no paid tiers
enrichment.headquarters: None; maintained as an open-source project on GitHub
enrichment.seo_title: CPU-X Review 2026: Free Linux Hardware Info Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CPU-X is a free open-source CPU-Z alternative for Linux and FreeBSD. See features, interfaces, supported languages, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CPU-X is a free, open-source system profiling and hardware monitoring application for GNU/Linux and FreeBSD, developed as a spiritual counterpart to CPU-Z, the well-known Windows hardware diagnostic tool from CPUID. The project is hosted on GitHub under the maintainer name TheTumultuousUnicornOfDarkness and is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3.0 (GPL-3.0), meaning it is free to use, modify, and redistribute. CPU-X gathers detailed information about a system's central processor (CPU), motherboard and chipset, random access memory (RAM), and graphics processing unit (GPU), presenting details such as processor model, core and thread counts, clock speeds, cache sizes, memory type and timings, and motherboard model and BIOS information. The application supports multiple interface modes to suit different environments: a graphical mode built with GTK for desktop use, a text-based mode built with NCurses for terminal and headless environments, and a non-interactive dump mode that outputs system information directly to the command line, which is useful for scripting or diagnostic logging. One of CPU-X's distinguishing technical features is its daemon mode, in which a small helper component runs with root/administrator privileges to gather hardware information that requires elevated access, then passes that data back to the user interface running under a normal, non-privileged user account, balancing functionality with security best practices. CPU-X supports both x86 systems (32-bit and 64-bit) and, since version 5.1.0, ARM architectures in both 32-bit and 64-bit variants, extending its usefulness to a wider range of hardware including single-board computers and ARM-based Linux devices. The codebase is written primarily in C and C++, built using the CMake build system, and the project has accumulated more than 2,800 commits, roughly 2,600 GitHub stars, and 155 forks as of its most recent releases, with the latest stable version being v5.4.0. CPU-X is distributed through multiple channels to simplify installation across different Linux distributions, including as a Flatpak on Flathub (available since v5.0.2), as a self-contained AppImage that requires no package manager or system modification, and through native packages in various distribution repositories. The application supports internationalization through the gettext translation framework and the Weblate collaborative translation platform, and has been localized into a large number of languages, with Fedora's localization statistics showing CPU-X translated into 38 languages as of a recent release, including major European and Asian languages alongside English. As a community-maintained open-source project rather than a commercial company, CPU-X has no headquarters, no pricing tiers, and no company size in the traditional sense; its ongoing development depends on volunteer maintainers and contributors, and the project's GitHub page has periodically noted uncertainty about long-term maintenance milestones. CPU-X is commonly recommended in Linux hardware guides and comparison articles as the closest equivalent to CPU-Z for users who need to verify processor specifications, diagnose hardware, or document system configuration on Linux and FreeBSD systems, particularly for enthusiasts building or troubleshooting PCs who previously had to rely on separate command-line tools like lscpu, dmidecode, or lspci to gather the same information manually.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is CPU-X
paragraphs: CPU-X is a free, open-source hardware information tool for GNU/Linux and FreeBSD that consolidates data about the CPU, motherboard, memory, and graphics hardware into a single application, functioning as a Linux equivalent to CPU-Z on Windows.

It is released under the GPL-3.0 license and maintained by volunteer contributors on GitHub rather than by a commercial company, which means there is no pricing, subscription, or paid tier associated with the software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Interface Modes: GTK, NCurses, and Dump
paragraphs: CPU-X offers three ways to view system information: a graphical GTK interface for desktop use, a text-based NCurses interface for terminal environments, and a non-interactive dump mode that prints system data directly to standard output for scripting or logging purposes.
A daemon mode allows CPU-X to gather hardware data that requires elevated privileges through a small root-level helper process, while the main interface continues running under a normal user account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Installation and Supported Platforms
paragraphs: CPU-X supports x86 systems in both 32-bit and 64-bit variants, and since version 5.1.0 has added support for ARM architectures in 32-bit and 64-bit forms, broadening its use to single-board computers and ARM-based Linux devices.

It can be installed as a Flatpak from Flathub, downloaded as a self-contained AppImage that requires no package manager, or installed through native packages available in many Linux distribution repositories.

features.title: Detailed CPU information
features.description: Reports processor model, codename, core and thread counts, clock speeds, and cache sizes.

features.title: Motherboard and chipset data
features.description: Displays motherboard model, chipset, and BIOS information.

features.title: Memory (RAM) details
features.description: Shows memory type, size, timings, and module specifications.

features.title: GPU information
features.description: Collects graphics processing unit details alongside CPU, motherboard, and memory data.

features.title: Multiple interface modes
features.description: Offers GTK graphical mode, NCurses text-based mode, and a command-line dump mode for different use cases.

features.title: Daemon mode for privileged data
features.description: Runs a root-level helper component to safely gather data that requires elevated permissions, separate from the main user interface.

features.title: ARM and x86 support
features.description: Supports both x86 (32-bit and 64-bit) and ARM (32-bit and 64-bit) architectures.

features.title: Multiple installation formats
features.description: Available as a Flatpak, AppImage, or native distribution package, simplifying installation across different Linux setups.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPL-3.0
Supports both GTK graphical and NCurses text-based interfaces for different environments
Works on both x86 and ARM hardware
Multiple installation options including Flatpak and AppImage reduce dependency issues
Actively maintained with an established release history and community translations into dozens of languages
cons: Windows and macOS are not supported; it is Linux and FreeBSD only
Some hardware detection features require root/daemon access, which may raise permission concerns for some users
As a volunteer-maintained project, long-term development pace and roadmap can be less predictable than commercial software
Less polished visual design compared to some commercial system information tools
Detection accuracy can vary across less common or very new hardware compared to vendor-specific tools

faqs.question: What is CPU-X used for
faqs.answer: CPU-X is used to view detailed information about a Linux or FreeBSD system's CPU, motherboard, memory, and GPU, similar to what CPU-Z provides on Windows.

faqs.question: Is CPU-X free
faqs.answer: Yes, CPU-X is free and open-source software released under the GPL-3.0 license, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Does CPU-X work on Windows
faqs.answer: No, CPU-X is designed for GNU/Linux and FreeBSD; it does not run natively on Windows.

faqs.question: What interfaces does CPU-X offer
faqs.answer: CPU-X offers a GTK graphical interface, an NCurses text-based interface for terminals, and a command-line dump mode for scripting.

faqs.question: Does CPU-X support ARM processors
faqs.answer: Yes, since version 5.1.0, CPU-X supports ARM architectures in both 32-bit and 64-bit forms, in addition to x86.

faqs.question: How do I install CPU-X
faqs.answer: CPU-X can be installed as a Flatpak from Flathub, downloaded as a self-contained AppImage, or installed through native packages available in many Linux distribution repositories.

faqs.question: Why does CPU-X need root access for some features
faqs.answer: CPU-X uses an optional daemon mode where a small helper component runs with elevated privileges to collect hardware data that is not accessible to a normal user account, then passes it to the main interface.

faqs.question: Who maintains CPU-X
faqs.answer: CPU-X is maintained by volunteer open-source contributors on GitHub under the maintainer TheTumultuousUnicornOfDarkness, rather than by a commercial company.

use_cases.title: Verifying hardware specifications on Linux
use_cases.description: Users confirm exact CPU, motherboard, and memory specifications on a Linux or FreeBSD system without relying on multiple separate command-line utilities.

use_cases.title: Hardware troubleshooting and diagnostics
use_cases.description: System administrators and enthusiasts use CPU-X to diagnose hardware issues by reviewing detailed CPU, memory, and motherboard data in one place.

use_cases.title: Documenting system configuration
use_cases.description: Users run CPU-X in dump mode to output hardware specifications to a file or log for documentation, support tickets, or asset inventory purposes.
use_cases.audience: IT support and asset management teams

use_cases.title: ARM device and single-board computer inspection
use_cases.description: Users of ARM-based Linux devices, including single-board computers, use CPU-X to inspect hardware details on architectures beyond standard x86 desktops.

enrichment.short_description: CPU-Z is a free system information and diagnostic utility for Windows, developed by the French company CPUID, that reports detailed specifications for a computer's processor, motherboard, memory, and graphics card. It provides real-time measurements of core clock speeds and memory frequency, and includes a hardware validation feature that generates a shareable link summarizing a system's specs. CPU-Z is widely used by PC enthusiasts, overclockers, and buyers verifying hardware authenticity.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free freeware, no paid tiers or subscriptions
enrichment.seo_title: CPU-Z Review 2026: Free Windows Hardware Info Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CPU-Z by CPUID reports CPU, motherboard, memory, and GPU specs on Windows. See features, validation tool, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CPU-Z is a free system information and diagnostic utility for Windows created by Franck Delattre, whose work later formed the basis of CPUID, a software company he founded in 2007 and headquartered in Dunkerque, France. CPU-Z itself predates the formal company, with its first versions released in the early 2000s as a lightweight tool for reporting detailed hardware specifications, and it has since become one of the most widely used free diagnostic utilities in the PC enthusiast and overclocking communities. The application gathers and displays information across several categories, organized into tabs within its interface: the CPU tab shows processor name and number, codename, manufacturing process, package type, core voltage, and multiple levels of cache; the Mainboard tab reports motherboard manufacturer, model, and chipset, along with BIOS details; the Memory and SPD tabs show installed RAM type, total size, timings, and per-module specifications read directly from each memory stick's Serial Presence Detect (SPD) data; and the Graphics tab reports the installed GPU model and specifications. Beyond static specifications, CPU-Z provides real-time monitoring of each CPU core's internal operating frequency and current memory frequency, which is useful for verifying that overclocking settings are being applied correctly or that a system is running at its rated speeds. A widely used feature is CPU-Z's hardware validation tool, which generates a unique, shareable web link summarizing a system's full hardware configuration at the moment of validation; this is commonly used to prove a specific hardware configuration for online forum discussions, benchmark submissions, hardware trading or resale listings, or overclocking competition verification. CPU-Z is distributed entirely free of charge as freeware, with no premium tier, subscription, or paid unlock, and CPUID's revenue instead comes from other commercial and enterprise-focused products in its lineup, such as PC Wizard, HWMonitor, and enterprise hardware inventory tools. CPU-Z supports a broad range of Windows versions, from older releases such as Windows XP and Vista through Windows 7, 8, 10, and full support for Windows 11, and both 32-bit and 64-bit editions are available for download. CPUID also publishes a CPU-Z mobile app for Android that reports equivalent hardware information for smartphones and tablets, extending the CPU-Z brand beyond desktop PCs. Because CPU-Z reads hardware information directly rather than relying on manufacturer marketing specifications, it is frequently used to verify that a purchased or pre-built PC actually contains the components it was advertised with, to confirm memory is running at its rated speed and timings, and to diagnose potential hardware issues such as underperforming components. As a long-standing, narrowly scoped freeware tool rather than a broad system utility suite, CPU-Z's popularity rests on its accuracy, minimal footprint, and status as a de facto standard reference tool that is frequently bundled or recommended alongside benchmarking software in reviews, buying guides, and enthusiast communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is CPU-Z
paragraphs: CPU-Z is a free Windows utility from CPUID that reads and displays detailed hardware specifications for a computer's processor, motherboard, memory, and graphics card, organized into dedicated tabs within a simple interface.

It reports both static specifications, such as processor codename and cache sizes, and real-time data, such as current core clock speed and memory frequency, making it useful for both identification and live monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Hardware Validation Links
paragraphs: CPU-Z's validation feature generates a unique web link that summarizes a system's full hardware configuration at a specific point in time, which users can share as proof of their setup.
This feature is commonly used in enthusiast forums, benchmark submissions, and hardware resale or trading listings to demonstrate that a system matches its claimed specifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Platform and Version Support
paragraphs: CPU-Z supports a wide range of Windows versions, from Windows XP and Vista through Windows 7, 8, 10, and Windows 11, in both 32-bit and 64-bit editions.
CPUID also offers a CPU-Z app for Android that reports equivalent hardware information on mobile devices, extending the tool beyond desktop PCs.

features.title: Detailed CPU specifications
features.description: Displays processor name, codename, manufacturing process, package, core voltage, and multiple cache levels.

features.title: Motherboard and chipset info
features.description: Reports motherboard manufacturer, model, chipset, and BIOS details.

features.title: Memory and SPD data
features.description: Shows RAM type, total capacity, timings, and per-module specifications read from SPD data.

features.title: Graphics card detection
features.description: Reports the installed GPU model and core specifications alongside CPU and memory data.

features.title: Real-time frequency monitoring
features.description: Measures each CPU core's live operating frequency and current memory frequency for overclocking verification.

features.title: Hardware validation links
features.description: Generates a shareable web link summarizing a system's full hardware configuration for public verification.

features.title: Broad Windows version support
features.description: Runs on Windows XP through Windows 11, with both 32-bit and 64-bit versions available.

features.title: Android companion app
features.description: CPUID offers a CPU-Z app for Android that reports equivalent hardware details on mobile devices.

pros: Completely free with no paid tier or subscription required
Trusted, long-standing reputation among PC enthusiasts and overclockers as an accuracy benchmark
Lightweight application with minimal system resource usage
Real-time monitoring supports overclocking verification, not just static specs
Validation links provide an easy way to share verified hardware configurations publicly
cons: Windows-only; no native macOS or Linux version (Linux users typically use CPU-X instead)
Interface is utilitarian and has not changed significantly in visual design over many years
No official paid version means no dedicated commercial support channel for CPU-Z itself
Focused narrowly on identification and monitoring, without broader system optimization or cleanup tools
Occasional detection lag for very new or unreleased hardware until CPU-Z is updated

faqs.question: What does CPU-Z do
faqs.answer: CPU-Z reports detailed specifications for a Windows computer's processor, motherboard, memory, and graphics card, and provides real-time monitoring of core and memory clock speeds.

faqs.question: Is CPU-Z free
faqs.answer: Yes, CPU-Z is completely free freeware published by CPUID, with no paid version or subscription.

faqs.question: Who makes CPU-Z
faqs.answer: CPU-Z is made by CPUID, a French software company headquartered in Dunkerque, France, founded by Franck Delattre.

faqs.question: What is CPU-Z validation used for
faqs.answer: CPU-Z validation generates a shareable web link summarizing a system's full hardware configuration, commonly used to prove a hardware setup in forums, benchmarks, or resale listings.

faqs.question: Does CPU-Z work on Windows 11
faqs.answer: Yes, CPU-Z is fully supported on Windows 11, as well as earlier versions including Windows 7, 8, and 10.

faqs.question: Is there a CPU-Z app for phones
faqs.answer: Yes, CPUID publishes a CPU-Z app for Android that reports equivalent hardware information for smartphones and tablets.

faqs.question: Is there a Linux version of CPU-Z
faqs.answer: No, CPU-Z is Windows-only; Linux users typically use CPU-X, an open-source tool designed to provide similar functionality on Linux and FreeBSD.

faqs.question: What is CPU-Z commonly used for
faqs.answer: CPU-Z is commonly used to verify that a PC's actual hardware matches its advertised specifications, confirm memory is running at rated speed, and monitor clock speeds during overclocking.

use_cases.title: Verifying PC hardware before or after purchase
use_cases.description: Buyers use CPU-Z to confirm that a pre-built or used PC actually contains the processor, memory, and graphics card it was advertised with.

use_cases.title: Overclocking verification
use_cases.description: Overclockers use CPU-Z's real-time frequency monitoring and validation links to confirm and document stable overclocked settings.

use_cases.title: Hardware troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Technicians use CPU-Z to quickly identify installed components when diagnosing performance issues or planning upgrades.
use_cases.audience: IT technicians and hardware repair shops

use_cases.title: Sharing verified system specs
use_cases.description: Forum users and benchmark submitters use CPU-Z validation links to publicly document their exact hardware configuration.
use_cases.audience: PC enthusiast communities and benchmark submitters

enrichment.short_description: Cradlepoint is a provider of cellular and 5G wireless edge networking hardware and cloud-managed software, offering routers and adapters for enterprise branch, mobile, and IoT connectivity. Founded in 2006 and headquartered in Boise, Idaho, the company was acquired by Ericsson in 2020 for an enterprise value of about 1.1 billion USD and now operates as part of Ericsson's enterprise business. Its NetCloud platform combines purpose-built routers with cloud management, security, and SD-WAN capabilities.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription-based NetCloud service plans bundled with hardware, sold primarily through partners and resellers with quote-based enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: Reseller-quoted subscription and hardware pricing; publicly listed NetCloud plan pricing has ranged from roughly 28.50 USD to over 1,000 USD depending on plan tier, term length, and hardware
enrichment.seo_title: Cradlepoint Review 2026: 5G Routers, NetCloud and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cradlepoint (Ericsson) provides 5G wireless edge routers and NetCloud cloud management. See features, plans, pros, cons, and FAQs for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cradlepoint is a wireless wide area network (WWAN) edge networking company founded in 2006 and headquartered in Boise, Idaho, United States, with additional development centers in Silicon Valley and India and international offices in the United Kingdom and Australia. The company was acquired by Ericsson on November 1, 2020, in a deal valuing Cradlepoint at an enterprise value of approximately 1.1 billion USD, and it now operates as part of Ericsson's enterprise wireless solutions business, marketed under names including Ericsson Cradlepoint and NetCloud by Ericsson. Cradlepoint's core business is cellular and 5G wireless edge networking: purpose-built routers, adapters, and gateways that use 4G LTE and 5G cellular connections as primary or backup wide area network links for branch offices, vehicles, IoT deployments, and temporary or pop-up locations, paired with a cloud-based management and orchestration platform called NetCloud. NetCloud Service centralizes device provisioning, configuration, monitoring, security policy enforcement, and lifecycle management for Cradlepoint's hardware fleet, and is organized into several product lines aligned to use case: NetCloud for Enterprise Branch, which supports advanced routing, dynamic path selection, SD-WAN integration, and Zero Trust-style security segmentation for multi-site branch networks using hardware like the E300, E400, and E3000 series routers; NetCloud for Small Branch, which offers simplified, template-driven configuration and basic firewalling for lower-complexity sites with faster deployment; NetCloud for Mobile, which targets in-vehicle and field-service connectivity using ruggedized routers designed for public safety, transportation, and utility fleets; and NetCloud for IoT, optimized for connecting distributed sensors and devices to the cloud with an emphasis on security and reliability at scale. NetCloud plans are typically sold as multi-year subscriptions bundled with hardware, support, and warranty coverage, and pricing is largely quote-based through Cradlepoint's partner and reseller network rather than published as flat public list prices; publicly available reseller pricing shows a wide range depending on plan tier, hardware model, and subscription term, spanning from roughly the high 20s to over 1,000 USD per unit depending on configuration. Higher NetCloud Advanced tiers add features such as application traffic analytics, application-aware firewalling, and URL content filtering on top of base connectivity and management. Cradlepoint has also worked with Ericsson on network slicing proof-of-concept demonstrations aimed at showing how 5G network slicing can unlock differentiated connectivity tiers for enterprise customers, reflecting the strategic rationale behind Ericsson's acquisition: combining Ericsson's mobile network infrastructure and 5G expertise with Cradlepoint's enterprise-facing edge hardware and cloud management software. Cradlepoint is generally positioned as a leader in the cloud-delivered 4G and 5G wireless WAN edge category, competing with vendors such as Cisco Meraki, Peplink, and Sierra Wireless, and is most commonly selected by organizations that need reliable, centrally managed cellular connectivity for distributed branch locations, mobile workforces, or IoT deployments where wired broadband is unavailable, unreliable, or needed only as a backup link.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cradlepoint and NetCloud
paragraphs: Cradlepoint builds cellular and 5G routers and gateways paired with NetCloud, a cloud-based platform for provisioning, monitoring, and securing that hardware fleet at scale across branch, mobile, and IoT deployments.

Since its 2020 acquisition by Ericsson, Cradlepoint operates as part of Ericsson's enterprise business, combining Ericsson's mobile network expertise with Cradlepoint's edge hardware and cloud software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cradlepoint Product Lines
paragraphs: Cradlepoint organizes its offering around use cases: Enterprise Branch for advanced multi-site routing and SD-WAN, Small Branch for simplified low-complexity deployments, Mobile for in-vehicle and field connectivity, and IoT for distributed sensor and device connectivity.

Each product line pairs specific router hardware, such as the E300, E400, and E3000 series for branch deployments, with a matching NetCloud subscription tier that determines available management, security, and analytics features.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Cradlepoint Pricing Works
paragraphs: Cradlepoint does not publish simple flat pricing; NetCloud plans are sold as subscriptions bundled with hardware, support, and warranty, typically through a network of partners and resellers, with final pricing depending on hardware model, plan tier, and contract term.
Reseller-listed prices for individual NetCloud licenses and hardware bundles vary widely, reflecting the enterprise-focused, quote-driven nature of Cradlepoint's go-to-market model.

features.description: In-vehicle routers are designed for public safety, transportation, and field-service fleets requiring durable hardware.

features.description: NetCloud for IoT connects distributed sensors and devices to the cloud with an emphasis on security and reliability.

features.description: NetCloud subscriptions bundle cloud management, customer support, and lifecycle warranty with purpose-built hardware.

pros: Backed by Ericsson's global network infrastructure expertise and resources since the 2020 acquisition
Purpose-built hardware and cloud management designed specifically for cellular WAN edge use cases
Broad product range covering branch, mobile, and IoT connectivity needs
Centralized NetCloud management simplifies operating large distributed router fleets
Established leader in the cloud-delivered 4G/5G wireless WAN edge category
cons: Pricing is not transparently published; customers must go through partners or resellers for quotes
Requires ongoing NetCloud subscription costs in addition to hardware purchase
Enterprise-oriented positioning may be more complex or costly than needed for very small deployments
Being part of Ericsson means product direction is tied to a much larger corporate parent's strategy
Advanced security and analytics features are gated behind higher NetCloud Advanced tiers

faqs.question: What does Cradlepoint do
faqs.answer: Cradlepoint provides cellular and 5G wireless edge networking hardware, such as routers and adapters, paired with its NetCloud cloud management platform for branch, mobile, and IoT connectivity.

faqs.question: Is Cradlepoint owned by Ericsson
faqs.answer: Yes, Ericsson acquired Cradlepoint in November 2020 for an enterprise value of about 1.1 billion USD, and Cradlepoint now operates as part of Ericsson's enterprise business.

faqs.question: What is NetCloud
faqs.answer: NetCloud is Cradlepoint's cloud-based platform for provisioning, monitoring, securing, and managing its fleet of cellular routers and adapters across locations.

faqs.question: How much does Cradlepoint cost
faqs.answer: Cradlepoint does not publish simple flat pricing; NetCloud subscriptions bundled with hardware are typically quoted through partners and resellers, with publicly seen prices ranging from roughly 28.50 USD to over 1,000 USD depending on tier and term.

faqs.question: Where is Cradlepoint headquartered
faqs.answer: Cradlepoint is headquartered in Boise, Idaho, United States, with development centers in Silicon Valley and India and offices in the UK and Australia.

faqs.question: What hardware does Cradlepoint make
faqs.answer: Cradlepoint makes cellular and 5G routers and gateways, including the E300, E400, and E3000 series for branch deployments, plus ruggedized routers for mobile and vehicle use.

faqs.question: Who uses Cradlepoint products
faqs.answer: Cradlepoint is used by enterprises needing branch office connectivity, field service and public safety fleets needing in-vehicle connectivity, and organizations deploying distributed IoT devices.

faqs.question: When was Cradlepoint founded
faqs.answer: Cradlepoint was founded in 2006 in Boise, Idaho, before being acquired by Ericsson in 2020.

use_cases.title: Branch office WAN connectivity
